attachment.content:
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- userinterface-wiki: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- code-edit: Use Fast Apply (edit_file) for precise edits without reading full files.
- mastra: Comprehensive Mastra framework guide. Teaches how to find current documentation, verify API signatures, and build agents and workflows. Covers documentation lookup strategies (embedded docs, remote docs), core concepts (agents vs workflows, tools, memory, RAG), TypeScript requirements, and common patterns. Use this skill for all Mastra development to ensure you're using current APIs from the installed version or latest documentation.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- teach-impeccable: One-time setup that gathers design context for your project and saves it to your AI config file. Run once to establish persistent design guidelines.
- onboard: Designs and improves onboarding flows, empty states, and first-run experiences to help users reach value quickly. Use when the user mentions onboarding, first-time users, empty states, activation, getting started, or new user flows.
- explore: Use this skill when the user asks you something that requires searching a code base
- normalize: Audits and realigns UI to match design system standards, spacing, tokens, and patterns. Use when the user mentions consistency, design drift, mismatched styles, tokens, or wants to bring a feature back in line with the system.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- harden: Improve interface resilience through better error handling, i18n support, text overflow handling, and edge case management. Makes interfaces robust and production-ready. Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, or fix overflow and i18n issues.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- feature-research: Research existing architecture before implementing a complex feature.
- extract: Extract and consolidate reusable components, design tokens, and patterns into your design system. Identifies opportunities for systematic reuse and enriches your component library. Use when the user asks to create components, refactor repeated UI patterns, build a design system, or extract tokens.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context.
- arrange: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- tanstack-start-best-practices: TanStack Start best practices for full-stack React applications. Server functions, middleware, SSR, authentication, and deployment patterns. Activate when building full-stack apps with TanStack Start.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- presentation-design: Design and evaluate presentations that communicate effectively. Use when designing a presentation, creating slides, getting presentation feedback, structuring a talk, or reviewing slides. Keywords: presentation, slides, talk, PowerPoint, Keynote, reveal.js.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Add PDF export button to settlement view

content.text:
This is a server component (Next.js App Router). I'll use the simplest robust approach: a small client-side "Export PDF" button that triggers `window.print()` against a print-styled section. That's reliable, doesn't add a heavy dependency, and gives a proper PDF via the browser's "Save as PDF" dialog.

Let me check existing button styles and look at the package.json to confirm we're in Next.js.

aiTitle: Add PDF export button to settlement view

content.text: [Request interrupted by user]
window.print() isn't great, I just want a nice pdf of whats in Settlement worksheet card component, add the download button to top right of that component
Good call — a real PDF of just the worksheet card. Let me pick a generation approach.

questions.question: How would you like the PDF generated?

options.label: jsPDF + autoTable (Recommended)
options.description: Adds ~80kb client dep. Builds a clean text-based PDF from the worksheet data (not a screenshot). Crisp, selectable text, small file, deterministic layout.

options.label: Server-side PDF via @react-pdf/renderer
options.description: Heavier dep but fully controllable. Renders on the server as a route handler — click downloads a `.pdf`. Best fidelity to a designed layout.

options.label: html2canvas + jsPDF
options.description: Screenshots the worksheet card DOM and embeds in PDF. Pixel-perfect match to what's on screen but larger file, raster text (not selectable), heavier deps.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "How would you like the PDF generated?"="jsPDF + autoTable (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

options.label: Server-side PDF via @react-pdf/renderer

options.label: html2canvas + jsPDF

content.text:
Clean. Here's what shipped:

**New file** — [components/settle/export-worksheet-pdf.tsx](components/settle/export-worksheet-pdf.tsx)
- Client component using `jspdf` + `jspdf-autotable`
- Builds a text-based (selectable, crisp) PDF with three sections: box-office summary, worksheet steps (winner/net rows shaded to match the on-screen treatment), optional vs-comparison
- Footer with generation date + page number
- Filename: `settlement-{artist}-{date}.pdf`

**Wired into** [app/shows/[id]/settle/page.tsx:573-587](app/shows/[id]/settle/page.tsx#L573-L587) — button sits top-right of the Settlement worksheet `CardHeader` (the `flex justify-between` was already doing the layout work).

**Deps added** to package.json: `jspdf ^4.2.1`, `jspdf-autotable ^5.0.8`.

Worth eyeballing the actual PDF output — autoTable styling is easy to nudge once you see it (row padding, the winner-row tint, the courier amounts column). Want me to fire up `npm run dev` and step through a settled show to confirm it looks right?

content.text:
Two real bugs visible in the screenshot:

1. **"Amount" header misaligned in Box office table** — autoTable's `columnStyles[1].halign: "right"` only applies to *body* cells by default. The header text falls back to left-aligned. Need `headStyles.halign: "right"` or per-column header alignment.

2. **The weird `"` before "Fees" and "Expenses"** — those are quotation marks from `step.note`/`step.label`? No, looking closer: the steps that show `"` are the ones with `kind: "tier"`, and our code prepends 4 spaces as indent. Helvetica is rendering those leading spaces oddly with the `\n`-wrapped label. Actually — wait. The `"` glyph rendering is jsPDF's default behavior when the source string contains a Unicode character it doesn't have in its built-in font. Most likely culprit: the actual step labels contain en/em-dashes or curly quotes, or the indent is being interpreted oddly.

Let me check what the actual step labels look like for tier rows.

content.text:
Found it. Line 471: `"− Fees"` uses a Unicode minus sign (U+2212). Line 489 likely uses one too. Same with `×` (U+00D7) on lines 635, 583. jsPDF's built-in Helvetica (WinAnsi) doesn't support those, so they render as garbage glyphs that look like `"`.

The fix: substitute Unicode glyphs with ASCII-safe equivalents when building label cells. Also the `! ` looking text in the second row note is probably an em-dash (`—`, U+2014) — looking at the screenshot: "$1,114 over cap" — that's "!" which is the curly apostrophe being mangled.

content.text:
Confirmed — line 943 uses `→` (U+2192). That's the `!` glyph in the PDF.

The cleanest fix: a helper that sanitizes Unicode for jsPDF's WinAnsi-encoded Helvetica before passing strings into the PDF.

content.text:
Clean. Two fixes:

1. **Header misalignment** — autoTable's `columnStyles.halign` only applies to body cells. Added a `didParseCell` hook on each table that sets `halign: "right"` for the head row's amount column. Now applies to all three tables.

2. **Garbled `"` and `!` glyphs** — those were Unicode characters (`−` minus sign, `×` multiplication sign, `→` arrow, en/em dashes, curly quotes) hitting jsPDF's built-in Helvetica which only supports WinAnsi encoding. Added a `sanitizeForPdf()` helper that substitutes ASCII equivalents (`−`→`-`, `×`→`x`, `→`→`->`, etc.) and pipes every string from the deal math through it.

Also dropped the `"    "` leading-space indent for tier rows (which contributed to the weird wide-spaced look) in favor of proper `cellPadding.left` on tier cells.

Re-export from a settled show and the next render should look right.

lastPrompt: The top right acmount column header on first section is misalligned, also whats with the weird quites by fees and expenses
